digest.top_line: GEC-ESTRO expands APBI eligibility: now admits multifocal ≤2cm, pN1mi, all histologies, tumors ≤30mm; BRCA / <40yr / TNBC / EIC stay contraindicated.
digest.tldr: Breast carried the day's only item: GEC-ESTRO's refreshed APBI selection guideline (10 RCTs + 7 comparative studies, all ≥5yr f/u) widens good-candidate criteria and collapses to a binary good vs contraindicated scheme. No new outcome data; expert opinion fills the thin spots.

studies.name: GEC-ESTRO APBI Patient Selection Guidelines
studies.tldr: Selection criteria expanded to two tiers: good candidates (>40yr, ≤30mm, pN0/pN1mi, margins ≥2mm) vs contraindicated (BRCA, <40yr, TNBC, EIC, ≥pN1a).
details: 🔍 Evidence base: 10 prospective RCTs + 7 retrospective comparative studies, all ≥5yr median f/u

details.text: 🔍 APBI patient selection: good candidate vs contraindicated, by feature

details: 🔗 Expands 2010 GEC-ESTRO criteria: now admits multifocal (≤2cm), pN1mi, all histology types, tumors ≤30mm
🔍 Binary scheme only, good candidate vs contraindicated; no intermediate/cautionary category presented
⚠️ Recommendations supplemented by expert opinion where trial evidence is thin; no new outcome data reported
studies.significance: The expansion is the read: APBI now reaches multifocal disease within 2cm, pN1mi nodes, all histology types, and tumors up to 30mm, a wider eligible pool than the restrictive 2010 criteria. Moves who you offer partial-breast RT to at simulation, and where whole-breast stays the safer call.
studies.monday_clinic: In early breast cancer after lumpectomy with borderline-eligibility features (multifocal within 2cm, pN1mi, or DCIS with ≥2mm margins), this supports offering APBI where 2010 criteria excluded them; it does not extend to BRCA carriers, age <40, TNBC, or ≥pN1a nodal disease.
open_questions: Long-term local control under expanded criteria (multifocal, pN1mi)
Optimal APBI dose/fractionation across the broader eligible population

related_trials.brief_title: Risk-Stratified Hypofractionated Radiotherapy After Breast-Conserving Surgery for Early Breast Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This prospective study is designed to evaluate the feasibility, efficacy, and safety of risk-stratified hypofractionated radiotherapy after breast-conserving surgery in patients with early breast cancer.

interventions.name: Whole-breast irradiation and tumor bed boost

related_trials.eligibility_brief:

* ECOG performance status score of 0 to 2.
* Pathologically confirmed breast cancer, including invasive carcinoma or ductal carcinoma in situ.
* Clinical stage cT0-2N0M0.
* Underwent breast-conserving surgery.
* Negative surgical margins greater than 2 mm.

related_trials.brief_title: A Randomized Trial of Five Fraction Partial Breast Irradiation (RAPID2)
related_trials.brief_summary: The primary objective of this study is to determine in women with node negative BC ≤3cm in size, if PBI compared to WBI, both given once-a-day over 1 week following BCS, is non-inferior for LR and reduces adverse cosmesis. The primary outcomes are LR and patient-assessed cosmesis at 3 years post randomization.

related_trials.brief_title: PBI for Breast in Situ Carcinoma of Intermediate Low Risk As Local Adjuvant Treatment
related_trials.brief_summary: Screening and advances in breast imaging led to a continuous increase of Ductal Carcinoma in situ (DCIS) diagnosis. Whole breast radiotherapy was reported to be effective in reducing the risk of local recurrence in all analyzed patients and tumor characteristics. In order to de-escalate treatment in low and intermediate DCIS, it is possible to investigate the role of partial breast irradiation (PBI). papers.title: Patient selection for accelerated partial breast irradiation (APBI) after breast-conserving surgery: Updated evidence-based recommendations of the Groupe Européen de Curiethérapie-European Society for Therapeutic Radiology and Oncology (GEC-ESTRO) Breast Cancer Working Group.
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: To update recommendations on patient selection criteria for accelerated partial breast irradiation (APBI) based on available clinical evidence supplemented by expert opinions.

METHODS AND MATERIALS: Between 2010 and 2024, a systematic search of the PubMed, Medline, Scopus and Cochrane database identified 618 articles using the keywords "accelerated partial breast irradiation" and "APBI". This search was complemented by reviewing the reference lists of articles and manual reviewing of relevant conference abstracts and book chapters. Of these, ten prospective randomized clinical trials and seven retrospective comparative studies with a minimum median follow-up time of five years were identified. The authors reviewed the clinical evidence published on APBI, supplemented it with relevant clinical and pathological studies on breast-conserving therapy, and then formulated the recommendations presented in this manuscript.

RESULTS: Based on published new clinical evidence, the GEC-ESTRO Breast Cancer Working Group recommends two categories as guidelines for selecting patients eligible for APBI: (1) low-risk group representing good candidates for APBI including patients ageing > 40 years with unifocal or multifocal within 2 cm, pTis,T1-2 (≤30 mm) pN0 or pN1mi, all histology types of breast cancer without the presence of an extensive intraductal component (EIC), without extensive lympho-vascular invasion (LVI) and with negative surgical margins for invasive tumors (≥2 mm for DCIS), (2) high-risk group, for whom APBI is considered contraindicated including patients with BRCA 1-2 mutations or ageing < 40 years; having positive margins for invasive tumor (<2 mm for DCIS), and/or multicentric or large (>30 mm), and/or triple negative tumours, and/or EIC positive, and/or extensive lympho-vascular invasion (LVI) or macrometastatic positive lymph nodes (≥pN1a) or unknown axillary status (pNx).

CONCLUSIONS: Based on emerging clinical evidence, the 2010 GEC-ESTRO APBI patient selection criteria can be significantly expanded, meaning that in the future, more patients may receive APBI as a part of routine clinical practice.
